macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 20 and the Mishna on Two Oaths That Are Four

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and orientation to the daf
The Mishna: Two oaths regarding future action (eating and not eating) and two regarding past action
Rabbi Akiva's position: liability for consuming even the smallest amount
Rashi's explanation: focus on words as the basis for obligation regardless of quantity

macro_segments.title: Context and Linguistic Meaning in Oaths

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's opening question: does the word 'I will eat' always mean affirmative intent?
Abaye's position: context determines meaning; pressure to eat reverses the sense
Distinguishing cases with pressure versus without pressure
Rav Ashi's alternative reading: phonetic contraction of 'will not eat'

macro_segments.title: Technical Language of Oaths: Mivta, Issur, and Mifas

micro_segments.title: Introduction: mivta as a technical term derived from the biblical verse
Issur as a technical term and its relationship to status changes
Mifas (grabbing/association): definition and distinction from simple language
The Baraita's formulation and the debate between Abaye and Rava

macro_segments.title: Abaye vs. Rava: Whether Mifas (Association) Constitutes a Binding Oath

micro_segments.title: Abaye's position: mifas is like uttering an oath itself (kemotzi shevu'a mipiv dami)
Explanation of Tosafot's reading: mifas works only if it includes action-language
Illustration with apples: direct oath versus association versus double-association
Rava's position: mifas is not like uttering an oath; hatfasa works for nedarim but not shevu'ot

macro_segments.title: Baraita on the Forbidden Nature (Issur) in Torah and Hatfasa in Nedarim

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: defining issur in Torah through a case of hatfasa (object association)
The case: making wine and meat forbidden on a day similar to a day already forbidden
Rava's reading: the Baraita describes a classic neder, not a proof about issur as technical term

macro_segments.title: Abaye vs. Rava: Whether Mifas (Association) Constitutes a Binding Oath (continued)

micro_segments.title: The conceptual distinction: nedarim relate to object-status; shevu'ot relate to action and God's witness

macro_segments.title: Baraita on the Forbidden Nature (Issur) in Torah and Hatfasa in Nedarim (continued)

micro_segments.title: The principle of davar hanadur: nedarim require association with pre-existing vowed objects
Application to Tzom Gedaliah: adding a neder layer on top of a rabbinic fast

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Position on Issur as Technical Term

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan adopts Rava's view that both mivta and issur are technical terms for oaths

topical_tags.term: shevu'ot shtayim she-hein arba (two oaths that are four)
kemotzi shevu'a mipiv dami (like uttering an oath from one's mouth)
